overview: Annotates text units from input documents with [Microsoft Translator](http://www.microsofttranslator.com) candidates and/or creates a TMX translation memory from them. Processes multiple text units in batches for significantly faster throughput compared to using the Leveraging Step with the Microsoft Translator Connector individually. Text units flagged as non-translatable are skipped. Requires a Microsoft Azure subscription key (v3 API).

azureKey.help:
Azure subscription key for the Microsoft Translator v3 API. If you previously had a key for the v2 API, the same key should work with v3.

Obtain a key from [Azure Cognitive Services pricing](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/pricing/details/cognitive-services/).
notes: The v2 API was retired on 2019-04-30. You must use the v3 API (supported from M38+).
You must respect Microsoft's Terms of Service. Commercial or high-volume usage requires a separate commercial license agreement.

category.help:
Optional identifier for a trained custom engine from Microsoft Translator Hub. Can be specified in three ways:

- **Direct ID**: Enter the engine identifier (called 'category' in Microsoft Translator Hub) directly
- **Keyword**: Use `@@@keyword@@@` format — requires a properties file in **Engine mapping**
- **Domain variable**: Use `@@@${domain}@@@` — replaced by the first ITS Domain annotation found on a text unit
category.values: Either a direct engine ID string, or a keyword in `@@@keyword@@@` format (e.g., `@@@travel@@@`, `@@@client1.tech@@@`). The keyword `${domain}` is replaced by the first ITS Domain annotation value.
notes: Only the **first** occurrence of the ITS Domain annotation affects engine selection. All batches translated before a domain annotation is found use the empty (default) category.

Because this step works in batches, segments *before* the first Domain annotation but within the same batch as the annotation will be translated with the engine specified by that domain. For example, with a buffer of 50 and domain first appearing at event 60: events 1–50 use the default engine, events 51–100 (including 51–59) use the domain engine.
examples: `@@@travel@@@` — looks up `travel.<LANG>` in the engine mapping file
`@@@${domain}@@@` — dynamically uses the ITS Domain annotation value as the keyword

configPath.help:
Path to a Java properties file mapping category keywords to Microsoft Translator Hub engine IDs. Each line has the format:

```
<keyword>.<LANGUAGE>=<engineID>
```

Where `<keyword>` is a case-sensitive string (no spaces, `=`, or `.`) matching the keyword in `@@@keyword@@@`, and `<LANGUAGE>` is the uppercase language code of the target locale.

A **fallback mechanism** applies: if `client2.law.DE` is not found, the step looks for `client2.DE`. If no custom engine is found, the generic Microsoft engine is used.
configPath.values: File path supporting variables `${rootDir}`, `${inputRootDir}`, and locale variables (`${srcLoc}`, `${trgLoc}`, etc.).

maxEvents.help: Number of text unit events to accumulate before sending a single batch query to the Microsoft Translator API. Larger buffers mean fewer API calls and faster processing, but the API imposes limits on the volume of text per request.
notes: The batch boundary affects which engine is used when ITS Domain annotations are present — segments before and after the first domain annotation within the same batch share the same engine.

maxMatches.help: Maximum number of translation candidates to retain per source text unit. Each match is stored as an annotation on the text unit.

threshold.help: Minimum score a translation match must have to be retained. Matches below this score are discarded.
threshold.values: Integer score (0–100). See the Microsoft Translator Connector documentation for how scores are computed from match degree and rating values.

onlyWhenWithoutCandidate.help: Only sends text units to Microsoft Translator if they do not already have a translation candidate from a previous step or the original document. Useful for avoiding redundant API calls when earlier steps (e.g., TM leveraging) have already provided matches.

annotate.help: Adds translation matches as annotations on the text units. These annotations can be consumed by downstream pipeline steps (e.g., leveraging or quality check steps). Existing annotations from previous steps are preserved.

makeTmx.help: Creates a TMX translation memory file from the Microsoft Translator results. If a file already exists at the specified path, it will be **overwritten**.

tmxPath.help: Output path for the generated TMX file. Only used when **Generate a TMX document** is enabled.
tmxPath.values: File path supporting variables `${rootDir}`, `${inputRootDir}`, and locale variables (`${srcLoc}`, `${trgLoc}`, etc.).

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this path to be used

sendTmx.help: When enabled, the generated TMX document replaces the filter events as the input passed to the next pipeline step. If disabled, the original filter events continue through the pipeline.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for TMX to exist

markAsMT.help: Marks generated TM entries as machine translation output. When enabled, the `creationId` attribute of TMX `<tu>` elements is set to `"MT!"`.

fillTarget.help: Copies the highest-scoring translation candidate into the target segment (if the target is currently empty). Only matches returned by Microsoft Translator are considered — candidates from other sources are ignored. The candidate must score at or above the **Fill threshold**.

fillTargetThreshold.help: Minimum score required for the best translation candidate to be copied into the target. If the best match scores below this value, the target is left empty.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this threshold to apply

useTargetPrefix.help: Prepends a text prefix to any translation candidate copied into the target. Useful for visually flagging MT output during review.

targetPrefix.help: Text string prepended to translations copied into the target segment. Only applied when **Add a prefix** is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for the prefix to be applied

examples: `!MT__` — default prefix to flag machine-translated content

limitations: The Microsoft Translator API has restrictions for high-volume usage — contact Microsoft for details.
Only the **first** ITS Domain annotation encountered in a batch affects engine selection; subsequent domain annotations are ignored.
The v3 API no longer supports the translation memory feature that was available in v2.
The v3 implementation (M38+) is minimal and does not support newer features such as profanity filtering.
See also the limitations documented on the Microsoft Translator Connector page.
processingNotes: Text units flagged as non-translatable are **not** sent for translation.
This step batches multiple text units per API call, making it significantly faster than using the Leveraging Step with the Microsoft Translator Connector.
Improving MT output can be done with post-processing steps — e.g., the Space Check Step can fix extra or missing spaces around inline codes.
The v2 API (Client ID / Client Secret) was retired on 2019-04-30. M38+ uses the v3 API with Azure subscription keys.
Batch boundaries interact with ITS Domain annotations — segments before the first domain annotation in a batch share the engine of that batch.

examples.title: Engine mapping properties file
examples.description: A properties file that maps category keywords and target languages to trained Microsoft Translator Hub engine IDs. The step uses a fallback mechanism: `client2.law.DE` falls back to `client2.DE` if the specific entry is not found.

examples.title: Using ITS Domain for dynamic engine selection
examples.description: Set the **Category** to `@@@${domain}@@@` to dynamically select a trained engine based on the ITS Domain annotation found in the content. The domain value is used as the keyword to look up in the engine mapping file. Only the first domain annotation encountered is used.
